beats.prompt: How good is the optimal look-then-leap rule? Match each scenario to its best success probability.

feedback.correct: Right — optimalCutoff(7).p = 29/70, optimalCutoff(4).p = 11/24, and as n → ∞ both the skip fraction r*/n and the success probability converge to 1/e ≈ 37%.
hints: For small n, use the look-then-leap formula p_n(r*); for huge n, both the threshold fraction and the win rate approach 1/e.
optimalCutoff(4) → r*=2, p=11/24; optimalCutoff(7) → r*=3, p=29/70.
Match: 7 candidates → 29/70, 4 candidates → 11/24, huge n → 1/e.

beats.prompt: Apartment hunting: there are 5 great places available, each gone forever if you don't commit on the spot. Which strategy gives you the best shot at landing the single best apartment?

options: take the first place you like
view a few, commit to none, then take the next place better than all of them
wait and take the very last place

take the first place you like.note: That's 'take the first' — it wins only when the best happens to arrive first, which is 1 in 5 times. No better than a blind pick.

view a few, commit to none, then take th.note: Exactly — look-then-leap. Scout the first ~37% (here: 2 apartments) as a benchmark, then commit to the next one that beats them all. This wins ≈ 43% of the time versus 20% blind.

wait and take the very last place.note: That's 'take the last' — wins only when the best is in the final spot: 1 in 5. Same as a blind guess.

interaction.title: One rule, many names
interaction.body: The secretary problem, the marriage problem, the sultan's-dowry problem, house-hunting, even where to park: all the same optimal-stopping rule — spend the first ~37% as scouts, then take the first thing better than everything you scouted.

feedback.correct: Different names, same math: spend ≈ 37% scouting, then commit to the first record. The constraint structure — sequential, irrevocable, want the single best — is identical.
hints: Secretary problem = hiring. Marriage problem = dating. House-hunting = apartments. All reduce to the same irrevocable-choice structure.
The only difference is how the items are labeled — the threshold formula is unchanged.
Read the card, then tap Continue.

beats.prompt: Five apartments arrive in a random order. The optimal rule: scout the first 2, then take the next one that beats them both. Watch it play out — drag the cutoff to explore.

feedback.correct: The look phase (positions 1–2, ranks 3 and 5) sets the benchmark at rank 3. Apartment #3 arrives at position 3 with rank 1 — it beats the benchmark immediately. That's the best apartment: a win.
hints: With cutoff 3, the look phase is positions 1–2 (no commitment); the leap phase starts at position 3.
Ranks seen in the look phase: 3 and 5 — the benchmark is rank 3. Position 3 has rank 1, which beats it.
Rank 1 is the best apartment, so committing here is a win.

beats.prompt: Confirm the optimal probability for a small hiring run.

beats.interviewNote: Caveats for the interview: (1) if 'good enough' beats 'the single best', the threshold shrinks; (2) if you can RECALL passed candidates, you can do better; (3) with a KNOWN value distribution (full-information / Cayley's problem) the optimal win rate is higher (~58%). The 1/e result is specifically the no-information, must-get-the-best case.
beats.prompt: One threshold, three domains. Each is the secretary problem in disguise — reveal the shared 1/e rule.

lenses.label: Hiring
lenses.body: Scout ~37% of applicants, then hire the next best-so-far.

lenses.label: Apartments
lenses.body: View ~37%, then take the next place that beats them all.

feedback.correct: All three share the 1/e threshold. The names change; the math doesn't — unless you relax 'must get the single best', allow recall, or know the value distribution.
hints: The shared structure: items arrive one at a time, decisions are irrevocable, you want the unique best.
Any problem matching that structure obeys the same 1/e threshold as n grows large.
The caveats (good-enough, recall, known distribution) each break one of those three constraints.

beats.prompt: As n grows, the optimal cutoff fraction r*/n and the success probability both converge to 1/e. Watch the convergence across group sizes.

feedback.correct: At n = 50, the optimal cutoff is r* = 19 (reject the first 18). The fraction 19/50 = 0.38 is already close to 1/e ≈ 0.368, and both curves keep tightening toward that limit.
hints: optimalCutoff(n) maximizes p_n(r); as n grows, r*/n → 1/e and p_n(r*) → 1/e.
The headline is the optimal r* for the largest listed n — here n=50 gives r*=19.

beats.prompt: Classic interview framing: 100 applicants seen one at a time in random order, decide on the spot, want only the single best. Optimal strategy: reject the first batch as scouts, then hire the next record. How many do you reject?

fields.label: Classic: 100 applicants, hire on the spot, want the best — how many to reject first?

feedback.correct: Reject the first 37 (r* = 38, so the look phase covers positions 1–37), then hire the next applicant who beats every scout. Success probability ≈ 1/e ≈ 36.8%.
hints: optimalCutoff(100).r = 38, so the look phase is r*−1 = 37 candidates observed but never hired.
Starting at position 38, you commit to the first candidate who beats the best of the 37 scouts.
The answer interviewers want: reject the first 37, then take the next record.

interaction.scenario: You will view 5 apartments in random order, deciding on the spot whether to take each one — a passed apartment is gone forever. You use the optimal look-then-leap rule. \(\text{(a)}\) How many apartments should you view-and-pass in the look phase \((r^*{-}1)\)? \(\text{(b)}\) What is your probability of landing the single best apartment?

fields.label: How many to view-and-pass first (look phase, r*-1)
Chance of landing the single best apartment

feedback.correct: Optimal \(r^* = 3\): view and pass the first 2 apartments, then take the next one that beats both. Success probability \(p_5(3) = \frac{13}{30} \approx 43\%\) — more than double the blind \(\frac{1}{5}\).
hints: optimalCutoff(5) gives r*=3, so the look phase is positions 1–2 (skip 2 apartments).

feedback.correct: One rule spans hiring, apartments, dating, and parking: scout ~37%, then commit to the first record. Caveats — good-enough threshold, recall, known distributions — each change the optimum but not the core insight. You've completed Optimal Stopping; the capstone interview beat is next.
hints: The 1/e threshold applies when: items arrive sequentially, decisions are irrevocable, and you want the single best.
Relax any of those three constraints and the math changes — but the look-then-leap intuition survives.
You've covered the full arc: blind 1/n → look-then-leap → 1/e → real-world applications.
